text.title: Volumes and Security
text.summary: Named volumes for persistent data, read-only filesystems with tmpfs for writable temp dirs, non-root users, dropped capabilities, and secrets via env_file rather than hardcoded in compose.yaml.

rule.statement: Use named volumes (postgres-data:) for all persistent data. Never use anonymous volumes (just /app/data) — they are hard to identify, back up, and clean up. Use bind mounts (./src:/app) only in development, not production.
rule.rationale: Named volumes appear in docker volume ls with meaningful names. Anonymous volumes are identified only by UUID. Bind mounts in production create host-path coupling and UID/GID permission mismatches between the container user and host user.

example.code:
services: db: volumes: - postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
volumes: postgres-data: driver: local

rule.statement: For production services: set user: "1000:1000" to run as non-root, read_only: true with tmpfs for /tmp and /var/run, cap_drop: [ALL], and security_opt: [no-new-privileges:true].
rule.rationale: Root containers can modify host files via volume mounts. Read-only filesystems prevent tampering with application binaries. Dropping all capabilities removes privileged operations that most app containers never need.

example.code: services: app: user: "1000:1000" read_only: true tmpfs: - /tmp:size=100M - /var/run:size=10M cap_drop: - ALL security_opt: - no-new-privileges:true

rule.statement: Use env_file: .env to load secrets. Never hardcode credentials in compose.yaml. The compose.yaml file should be safe to commit to version control; .env must be in .gitignore with a .env.example provided.
rule.rationale: Hardcoded credentials in compose.yaml are committed to version history and visible to anyone with repo access. env_file keeps secrets out of the repo entirely.

rule.statement: Configure log rotation on every service with the json-file driver: max-size: "10m", max-file: "5". Without rotation, container log files grow without bound and fill the host disk.
rule.rationale: Docker stores container logs as JSON files on the host. A verbose service with no log rotation can fill /var/lib/docker with gigabytes of log data.

example.code: services: app: logging: driver: "json-file" options: max-size: "10m" max-file: "5"
